data.title: 'I was shoveling sidewalks at 8 years old': I'm a 73-year-old boomer dad with two kids. Here's what I teach them about finance
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'My parents knew nothing about finance and investing, so my buddies and I had to figure it out on our own'

"My dad insisted I get a job before he'd help me buy a car, but I said I couldn't get a job without a car." (Photo subject is a model.)

Dear Quentin,

Thank you for your answer to the parents who are worried about their child inheriting $19,000 ("An older relative wants to give my daughter $19,000 when she turns 18. I said no. Who's right?"; published March 26, 2026).

I have a 30-year-old and a 34-year-old. I'm 73. Your suggestions were right on. My parents knew nothing about finance and investing, so my buddies and I had to figure it out on our own.

I did the best I could with my kids. They are both 1099 workers, and your points about when they turn 18 are absolutely critical. You've got to stay relevant because you do lose control.

One of my children refuses to learn about investing and wants me to handle everything. What happens after I'm gone? My other child is very savvy and plays the market with small amounts. I gave him $5,000 a few years ago to help kick-start him.

It sounds like the parents who wrote to you and worry about their 18-year-old having too much responsibility with $19,000 in her bank account have their hearts in the right place, but they don't realize their limits.

My daughter got into trouble with credit cards as a freshman. There's so much more that can go wrong. One thing they may not fully understand is how many avenues for financial disaster are out there for young people:

-- Filling out credit applications without understanding the implications.

-- All the things financial advisers can do - both good and bad.

-- Forgetting about liability insurance as part of an umbrella policy.

-- And more mundane tasks, such as how to choose the right car insurance.

When I was 8 years old, I was shoveling sidewalks. In my teens, I didn't understand my dad's concerns. He was fixated on my getting a job after school.

We went back and forth several times until he realized that we lived on Long Island and there was no public transportation for me to get to work. My dad insisted I get a job before he'd help me buy a car, but I said I couldn't get a job without a car.

He finally relented and gave me some money. Within days of getting the car, I had a job. That's another option: If she needs a car to work, this can be a way to leverage it - similar to how you're framing "starting" a business.

Anyway, that letter hit close to home.

A Septuagenarian Dad

Don't miss: 'I want safe returns': I'm 73 with $300,000 saved. I'm not interested in the stock market. What should I do?

You can email The Moneyist with any financial and ethical questions at qfottrell@marketwatch.com. The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.

If you really value something, after all, you will do your utmost to preserve it.

Dear Dad,

Thank you for your thoughtful letter and for sharing your story. I, perhaps, don't say that enough to people who write to this column and share their experience, strength and hope! Yours is the kind of letter that lifts my spirit on a Monday morning.

Whether you're shoveling snow, doing a paper round or studying long hours in high school and college to turn that C into a B, and that B into an A, it helps to build character, stamina and patience. You'll need all of those if you start saving in your 20s.

So how can you help a child who doesn't want to actively manage their finances? Automate everything from bills to 401(k) matches - and create a visual picture of the stages of their life and how their finances should grow. Big picture stuff can be just as helpful as the minutiae.

What you're describing - both with the parents who are worried about their daughter being given $19,000 and your discussion with your father about getting a car and finding a job - represents the same tension between parent and child.

The parent, as you have discovered, wants to promote fiscal responsibility and make sure that their child/children appreciate the value of a car, home, job and savings - and invest enough of their blood, sweat and tears so they can appreciate it.

If you really value something, after all, you will do your utmost to preserve it. That could involve everything from paying your credit card every month as a 20-something to paying your rent and mortgage on time as a 30- and 40-something.

Related: 'Several hurricanes have come close': I'm 73 and live in a mobile home in Florida. Do I ditch my $2,400 home insurance?

Saving for a snowy day

It's no coincidence that you mention you were shoveling snow at 8 years old. Your father made sure you earned your allowance, and you got your first sense of what it was like to contribute to the household and - critically - be a stakeholder.

That is probably the biggest lesson for all of us: being a stakeholder, paying our taxes and contributing to the wider community. It's also the one sticking point politically as well as personally: big government versus small government, parent versus child.

How much does a child contribute to a household? And how much does a parent give a child so they can have a head start in life, but just enough for them to go out and seek their fortune independently? How much do we contribute in taxes for the greater good?

This manifests itself in many ways, as seen through the Moneyist letters over the years. Families with millions of dollars sometimes wrestle with the burden that money will put on their children. Will they work as hard in college? Will it exacerbate addiction issues?

It also works the other way, as some parents want to give their child every financial opportunity they never had. This woman wrote to me a couple of years ago about giving her daughter $5 million so she can travel the world and enjoy life with abandon.

Don't miss: 'She has taken my inheritance': My mom bullied my grandmother to change her will and physically abused her. What can we do?

A fortune for nothing

"I would like to create a trust fund of $5 million for her and suggest she quit work and travel for a few years to see the world," she said. "Would this ruin her life - or transform it? I just worry that she does not have the same opportunities."

I felt like such a gift could turn her daughter's life upside down, and I suspected - rightly or wrongly - that this mother could have been trying to encourage her daughter to fulfill her dreams rather than the dreams of her daughter.

"Your daughter," I told her, "takes pride in her career and the years of studying it took to get there. Has she told you that she would like to give it up? How would she feel if you effectively told her to throw it away and travel the world?"

The hard truth is that, for many people, the happiest time of their lives was when they had very little, and everything was ahead: heartbreak, success, failure, wrong turns, recessions, pandemics, job loss, promotion and, finally, retirement.

The flatshare in your 20s, the first job when everything is known and that modest salary feels like a king's ransom. That's probably how you felt when you were shoveling snow as an 8-year-old kid. You were earning your keep and learning the value of a quarter.

The value of being skint

How can you help your kids? You can set up a trust and estate plan to ensure a smooth transition. Make sure passwords are accessible, and documents - wills, trusts, life-insurance policies, retirement funds - are organized for your children when the time comes.

Let me share a story with you. When I was a kid, my father used to do what you have endeavored to do with your kids: He didn't want me to think money grew on trees, but he did want me to believe that money could be found among the leaves.

"Look what I found when I was cleaning the yard?" he once said, holding up a 5-pound note. At that moment, I thought, "Man, if only I'd been cleaning that yard instead, I'd be 5 pounds richer right now." I may have taken a bigger interest in picking up litter after that.

Memory is a funny thing. It might have been moments, weeks or even years later when I realized that he had placed the money among the fallen leaves. It didn't matter. I got the message. Pick up a sweeping brush, even if no one asked you to.

Ideally, we take those familial lessons to the workplace. We endeavor to encourage younger colleagues to negotiate their salaries but we must always show the value we bring to the table. Inflation-related pay hikes reward time. In a low-hire, low-fire environment, the rest is up to us.

All these years later, I am doing what your father did, what my father did and what you are doing with your kids: passing on my own philosophy - work hard, save while you're young and enjoy every moment - and perspective.

There's a 5-dollar bill hidden in these pages if you choose to find it.

Related: 'This is a first-world problem': I can't roll over my $800,000 401(k) from my prior employer. What did I do wrong?

data.title: 'I feel overwhelmed': I'm 56 and only have $60,000 in my IRA. Is it too late for me?
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'My husband has a pension, but I worry that if he passes before me, I could be left with nothing'

"I really need guidance and I am motivated to invest wisely." (Photo subject is a model.)

Dear Quentin,

I have a SEP IRA with $60,000 in it. It's diversified in the S&P 500 SPX and has been performing pretty well. I started investing later in life, and I'm currently 56. My husband has a pension, but I worry that if he passes before me, I could be left with nothing - that's how his pension is structured. Because of this, I've been working on building something of my own so I'll have more than just Social Security.

I feel overwhelmed by all the advice online. I don't want to work with an Edward Jones or Charles Schwab (SCHW) adviser. (My current adviser tends to disregard any ideas that don't align with the S&P 500.) Someone suggested finding a financial adviser who won't steer me toward their own products. Where can I find someone like that? I really need guidance and I am motivated to invest wisely - I just need some direction.

Investor & Wife

Don't miss: I fell victim to an online dating scam. I'm $27,000 in debt. 'Without commenting on my obvious stupidity, how do I recover from this?'

You can email The Moneyist with any financial and ethical questions at qfottrell@marketwatch.com. The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.

An S&P 500 index fund gives you suitably diversified exposure to the stock market, but while it may have more liquidity, you will still have to pay taxes on withdrawals.

Dear Investor,

Before you do anything, confirm whether your husband's pension is single life, or if it has a joint and survivor inclusion.

Until then, keep your faith and keep doing what you're doing. You have at least 10 to 15 years of work left, and you are presumably entering (or have entered) your peak earning years. So this is the time to double down and maximize your 401(k) matches. The current SEP IRA contribution limit for individuals over 50 is the lesser of 25% of compensation or $72,000, according to the Internal Revenue Service. Unlike traditional or Roth IRAs, SEPs do not allow catch-up contributions for people age 50 or older like your good self.

You will benefit from compounding whether you put money into an S&P 500 index fund, SEP IRA or 401(k). With a Roth IRA, you get significant tax advantages, including tax-free growth and qualified withdrawals, and no required minimum distributions if you are the original owner. An S&P 500 index fund gives you suitably diversified exposure to the stock market, but while it may have more liquidity, you will still have to pay taxes on withdrawals. If you make an IRA withdrawal before the age of 591/2, you will have to pay a 10% early-withdrawal penalty.

You can still get CD and high-yield savings account (HYSA) rates of up to 4.2%, although there are often conditions on the amount you can invest. Still, that gives you a slight advantage on inflation, which is running at 2.4% amid ongoing concern that the Federal Reserve may not cut interest rates further due to stagflation fears, a rise in energy prices and the war with Iran. HYSAs are more liquid and withdrawals are limited to half a dozen per month. With CDs, you are committing to a set period of time; when you buy a CD, the rate does not change.

Advisers may invest your money in ETFs and/or mutual funds, actively manage your finances or give you tips and not even touch your money, and advise you on tax-efficient withdrawals. Find out whether your adviser candidate is a member of the Financial Industry Regulatory Authority, and whether or not they operate under a dual-registered model. As you approach 60, a moderate risk profile would be 60% stocks (including non-U.S. equities), 30% bonds and 10% in cash or cash equivalents - although many people are far more aggressive than that.

Fee-only fiduciary adviser vs. revenue-sharing

How they get paid will tell you a lot. A fiduciary is legally and ethically bound to act in your best interest, prioritizing you (in theory, at least) over their own self-interest. Fiduciaries must avoid conflicts of interest, and must disclose any potential conflicts. That said, ask any candidates if they have revenue-sharing arrangements with any of the products they recommend and/or if they receive commission. (Read more here.) You can contact the National Association of Personal Financial Advisors and look up the Securities and Exchange Commission adviser database.

Merrill Lynch offers these questions to ask your prospective adviser, should you wish to engage one: "Do you have a special area of expertise? What is your track record? How - and how often - will we communicate? How can you help me stay on track as I work toward my goals? Can you help me invest in a way that reflects my values? Will you call me if the markets get scary? How do I pay you for your work - and how much will it cost?" (It could cost 1% of assets under management or $3,000 for a one-off plan. In your case, I don't think it's necessary.)

The key is to have enough savings and/or work long enough so you're not forced to take withdrawals in a down market in the first years of retirement (known as the "sequence of return" risk). The reason: If you have to take income from your retirement fund before your RMDs hit, you're losing possibly decades of potential returns. Given your age, your RMD age is 75; the window after you retire and before you avail yourself of your RMDs is considered the sweet spot for converting your IRA into a Roth (while your income is low).

Your other decision relates to when to take Social Security. The longer you hold out, the better - especially if you expect to live into your 80s. If you retired at 62, a $1,000 benefit would be reduced by 30%, according to the Social Security Administration. Every year you delay claiming past "full retirement age" (age 67) will add 8% to that benefit until you reach 70. That may seem like a long way off now, and it is - but the older you get, the faster the years pass you by. (For the record, the median retirement savings for someone age 56 and over is $185,000.)

Keep on truckin', working and saving. And if your husband's pension has no survivor clause, consider a life-insurance policy to help fill that gap.
